topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years—a slightly too-wide grin, the same familiar, mocking eyes. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. Hesitantly, I clicked on it. It was Mark. The name tasted like ash. A message popped up: "Hey. Long time no talk. Thought we could catch up." My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, trembling slightly. Every instinct screamed at me to ignore it, to let the digital silence remain unbroken. But a faint, unwelcome flicker of curiosity, mixed with a deep-seated anxiety about what might be unearthed, pulled my thumb down. I reread the message three times, the words echoing hollowly in the silence of my room.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at my phone, the screen illuminating the pale landscape of my face. When the request from Kevin finally appeared, a wave of nausea washed over me. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the ache in my molars. I scrolled through my recent contacts, searching for an escape route, but there was only this—a ghost from a past I desperately wanted to erase. His profile picture was aggressively cheerful, a stark contrast to the tightness gripping my chest. *Why would he even want to connect now?* The thought was sharp, immediate. I felt a sudden urge to delete the application entirely, to bury the entire interaction under a thick layer of digital dust. Yet, my thumb moved, slow and deliberate, landing on the confirmation. A tremor ran through my hand as I hit send, a small, defeated motion.

[story 3]

The chime sounded loud in the otherwise empty apartment. I flinched, pulling my gaze away from the glowing rectangle. It was him. The familiar, irritatingly confident smile from years ago stared back at me. A hot flush crept up my neck, making my skin feel too tight, too exposed. I leaned back against the worn sofa, trying to physically shrink into the upholstery. The message was simple: an invitation to reconnect. My breath hitched. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor in my grip. It felt like walking back into a trap I’d spent years trying to bypass. My mind raced, cataloging every past slight, every moment of humiliation, and the resulting tension made my shoulders involuntarily hunch forward. I stared at the screen, paralyzed by the sheer weight of that unexpected digital tether.
